content.problemStatement:
In an attempt to reduce the growing population, Archer was asked to come up with a plan. Archer being as intelligent as he is, came up with the following plan:

If NNN children, with names C1,C2,...,CNC_{1}, C_{2}, ... , C_{N}C1​,C2​,...,CN​, are born to parents with names AAA and BBB, and you consider CCC to be the concatenation of all the names of the children, i.e. C=C1+C2+...+CNC = C_{1} + C_{2} + ... + C_{N}C=C1​+C2​+...+CN​ (where +++ is concatenation operator), then CCC should be a substring of one of the permutations of A+BA + BA+B.

You are given the task to verify whether the names parents propose to give their children are in fact permissible by Archer's plan or not.


Input Format
The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. TTT test cases follow. Each test case stats with a line containing two space separated strings AAA and BBB, denoting the names of the parents. The next line contains a single integer NNN denoting the number of children AAA and BBB are planning to have. Following this are NNN lines, the ithi^{th}ith line containing CiC_{i}Ci​, the proposed name for the ithi^{th}ith child.



Output Format
For each test case output a single line containing YESYESYES if the names are permissible by Archer's plan, otherwise print NONONO.
constraints: All strings consist only of lowercase English letters
The total length of all children names in a test case does not exceed length(A)+length(B)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with two space‑separated strings A and B – the parents' names (lowercase letters only).
- A line with an integer N – the number of children.
- N subsequent lines, each containing a string C_i – the i‑th child's name (lowercase letters only).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing YES if the concatenation of all children names can appear as a contiguous substring of some permutation of A+B, otherwise output NO.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: A+ B = "tommarvoloriddle". Children concatenation = "lordvoldemort". All required letters exist, so YES.\nCase 2: A+ B = "cheapup". Children concatenation = "heapcup" which is a permutation of the parents, so YES.\nCase 3: Parents lack the letter 't' needed for "batman", thus NO.
A+ B = "abcdef" and the only child name is exactly "abcdef", so the needed letters match perfectly.
A+ B contains only one 'a', but the child name requires two 'a's, therefore the condition fails.

solution.approach: The order of characters inside a permutation of A+B can be chosen arbitrarily. Therefore a substring of length |C| can contain any multiset of characters that is a subset of the multiset of A+B. Consequently the condition reduces to: for every letter 'a'‑'z', its count in the concatenated children string must not exceed its count in A+B. We simply count frequencies of all letters in A+B and in all C_i, compare them, and answer YES if all needed counts are ≤ available counts.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|A| + |B| + total length of all children) per test case, which is linear in the input size.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only two arrays of size 26 are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        A = next(it); B = next(it)
        freq = [0]*26
        for ch in A+B:
            freq[ord(ch)-97] += 1
        N = int(next(it))
        need = [0]*26
        for _ in range(N):
            child = next(it)
            for ch in child:
                need[ord(ch)-97] += 1
        ok = all(need[i] <= freq[i] for i in range(26))
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
